issues.title: App shell missing skip-to-content target and ARIA landmarks
issues.description: SkipToContent component is rendered but the main content area lacks an id='main-content' target and role='main' landmark. Screen reader users cannot skip navigation. RouteAnnouncer exists but the main content div has no landmark role.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, in the AppContent function (the component that renders the Sidebar + main content area): find the main content wrapper div that contains the Routes. BEFORE: The div wrapping the page content area has no id or role attributes, so the SkipToContent link has no valid target. AFTER: Add id='main-content', role='main', and aria-label='Page content' to the main content wrapper div. Also ensure the Sidebar nav element has role='navigation' and aria-label='Main navigation'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Lazy route loading has no user-visible fallback for chunk load failures
issues.description: lazyWithRetry silently retries 3 times then throws, but there is no ErrorBoundary wrapping the Suspense fallback for route-level chunks. If a chunk fails after retries, the user sees a blank screen or a generic React error instead of a helpful retry UI.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, in the AppContent function: find the Suspense component that wraps the Routes. BEFORE: The Suspense has a fallback (likely a Skeleton or spinner) but no ErrorBoundary wrapping it specifically for chunk load failures. If lazyWithRetry exhausts retries, the error bubbles up to the top-level ErrorBoundary which shows a generic crash screen. AFTER: Wrap the Suspense + Routes block in a dedicated ErrorBoundary that catches chunk load errors (error.message includes 'Loading chunk' or 'Failed to fetch dynamically imported module') and renders a centered card with: 'This page failed to load' message, a 'Reload Page' button that calls window.location.reload(), and a 'Go to Dashboard' link. Keep the existing top-level ErrorBoundary for other errors. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No responsive breakpoint handling for sidebar collapse on tablet/mobile
issues.description: The useMediaQuery hook is imported but the sidebar collapsed state is not automatically set based on viewport width. On mobile (< 768px), the sidebar should auto-collapse or be hidden with a hamburger toggle. Currently, mobile users see the full sidebar consuming screen width.
issues.prompt: In the file src/App.tsx, in the AppContent function: find where sidebarCollapsed state is managed (likely via useUiStore or local useState). BEFORE: The sidebar collapsed state is only toggled manually by the user. On mobile viewports (< 768px), the full sidebar renders and overlaps or squeezes the main content. AFTER: Add a useEffect that watches the useMediaQuery('(max-width: 768px)') result. When the viewport is mobile-sized, automatically set sidebarCollapsed to true. When the viewport returns to desktop size, restore the user's previous preference. Also add a hamburger menu button (visible only on mobile) that toggles the sidebar as an overlay with a semi-transparent backdrop. Ensure the sidebar overlay has aria-modal='true' and a close button. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: createScopedClient Proxy re-created on every query call causing unnecessary GC pressure
issues.description: createScopedClient creates a new Proxy wrapper around the supabase client on every invocation. In projectScopedQuery, this is called per query. The nested Proxy (one for client.from, one for the query builder) generates garbage on every data fetch. Should memoize by projectId.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/client.ts, in the createScopedClient function: BEFORE: A new Proxy is created every time createScopedClient is called, even if the projectId is the same. This means every query creates two new Proxy objects that are immediately eligible for GC. AFTER: Add a module-level WeakMap or Map cache (Map<string, DbClient>) keyed by projectId that stores the scoped client Proxy. If createScopedClient is called with the same projectId, return the cached Proxy. Add a clearScopedClientCache() export for use during logout or project switching. Cap cache size at 5 entries (LRU eviction) to prevent memory leaks in multi-project scenarios. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: API router pathMatches function vulnerable to parameter pollution in path segments
issues.description: The pathMatches function splits on '/' and matches :param segments to anything, but does not validate that param values are valid UUIDs for :projectId or :id segments. A crafted path like '/api/v1/projects/../../admin/rfis' could potentially match unintended routes due to the normalize function not stripping '..' traversal.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/v1/router.ts, in the pathMatches function: BEFORE: The function matches any string for :param segments and the normalize function only deduplicates slashes and removes trailing slashes but does not strip path traversal sequences like '..'. AFTER: In the normalize function, add a step that removes any '..' or '.' path segments after splitting on '/': filter out segments that are '' or '.' or '..'. In pathMatches, when a pattern segment starts with ':', validate that the actual segment matches a safe pattern (alphanumeric, hyphens, and UUIDs only: /^[a-zA-Z0-9-]+$/). Return false if the actual segment contains unsafe characters. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's routing and infrastructure layer is mature with a custom API gateway, versioned REST endpoints, and field-tested error recovery. SiteSync's infrastructure shows good TypeScript patterns (scoped client proxy, typed query keys, centralized error transforms) that match or exceed Procore's developer experience. However, the accessibility gaps (missing landmarks, no skip navigation target) and the lack of automatic mobile sidebar collapse would fail both WCAG audits and field usability tests. Fieldwire handles mobile layout transitions seamlessly with auto-collapsing navigation. The API versioning via v1 router is a strong enterprise-readiness signal that Buildertrend lacks entirely.
summary: The Infrastructure & Routing module demonstrates solid TypeScript architecture with typed query keys, centralized error transformation, scoped Supabase client proxying, and a versioned API router pattern. Scoring 57 overall, the module is held back by five key issues: missing ARIA landmarks making the app shell inaccessible to screen readers (P0 for any enterprise sale), no user-visible recovery when lazy-loaded route chunks fail after retries (users see blank screens), no automatic sidebar collapse on mobile breaking field usability, unnecessary Proxy object churn in the scoped client creating GC pressure on list-heavy pages, and a path traversal vulnerability in the API router's normalize function. Fixing these five issues would lift the score to the 68-72 range by addressing the accessibility, mobile-first, error handling, performance, and security dimensions simultaneously.
